evidence: Multiple runtime seam structs are dominated by function fields and nil-guard forwarding methods (for example `Runtime*Ops` families and default builders in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` lines 18-253 and 301-478), increasing call-surface without consolidating domain behavior.

`importerRuntime` and `startupInitRuntime`/`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` repeat the same callback-bag + default merge + requirement validation pattern (`goseg/importer/importer.go` lines 87-207, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` lines 109-212 and 244-350), indicating abstraction duplication across subsystems.

Global singleton defaults are still used directly by production code (`defaultEventRuntime` in `goseg/docker/events/events.go` lines 36-40, `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go` line 16, `defaultWiFiRuntimeService` in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` lines 24-39), which bypasses injected seams and creates dual abstraction paths.

The config model remains a broad embedded bag (`goseg/structs/configs.go` lines 18-25), while runtime layers continue exposing broad snapshot callbacks (`RuntimeSnapshotOps` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` lines 165-172, `c2cRuntime` settings snapshot in `goseg/main.go` lines 161-205), keeping feature boundaries implicit.

Urbit section update behavior is duplicated across two layers: `config/urbit.go` implements `UpdateUrbitSection` + `UrbitSectionMutator` with a large type-switch, while `docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` re-exposes another full set of per-section wrappers (`UpdateUrbitRuntimeConfig`, `UpdateUrbitNetworkConfig`, etc.) and delegates back to the same mutator path.

Session broadcasting responsibility is split and duplicated: `session/client_manager.go` has `sessionBroadcaster.broadcast`, while `session/connection_registry.go` also defines a near-identical `broadcast` loop over token/client buckets.

Transition orchestration in `shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` combines state read/write, alias validation, external service calls (`startram`, `click`, `docker`), and UI transition-step construction in the same functions (notably `buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps` and domain step builders), while `rectify/rectify.go` contains another transition mapping/reducer orchestration surface for related domains.

`structs/configs.go` uses a large manual `UnmarshalJSON` switch over many keys, while `config/urbit.go` uses another large switch (`UrbitSectionMutator`) for section dispatch; schema and routing logic are spread across separate imperative switch blocks.

The event broker explicitly reports enqueue failures (`ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`, `ErrTransitionBusFull`) in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`, but multiple production call sites ignore returned errors: `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` (lines 70, 362) and `goseg/importer/importer.go` (e.g., `Reset`, `publishImportStatus`, `publishImportError`, `publishUploadStatus`, `monitorExtractionProgress`).

Backup transition flow in `goseg/handler/ship/backup.go` calls `publishUrbitTransitionForBackupService(...)` repeatedly without checking its error, while other operations in the same functions do return errors, creating inconsistent failure behavior for user-visible transition signaling.

`goseg/structs/configs.go` custom `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` silently drops invalid field types via unchecked assertions like `u.UrbitVersion, _ = v.(string)` and `u.Click, _ = v.(bool)`, while `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` enforces strict typed parse failures (`invalid <field> value: <type>`), resulting in inconsistent error semantics across config ingestion paths.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes create high indirection while pushing behavior to callers instead of encapsulating domain operations.
evidence: `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitConfigOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeConfigOps` and others are primarily function-field bundles with thin pass-through helpers (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` lines 33-253).

`importerRuntime` contains 20+ callback fields plus explicit requirement tables to check nil handlers (`goseg/importer/importer.go` lines 87-197), showing orchestration logic depends on wiring completeness rather than cohesive interfaces.

`startupInitRuntime` and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` repeat the same pattern with descriptor arrays selecting callbacks (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` lines 48-68 and 285-315).
findings.suggestion: Introduce narrower role-oriented interfaces per workflow boundary (for example `ContainerLifecycle`, `UrbitConfigStore`, `StartupSubsystemRunner`) and replace multi-dozen callback structs with composed typed dependencies; keep only one seam layer per subsystem and delete duplicate requirement-table plumbing.

findings.summary: Global singleton runtimes bypass injected seams, creating competing execution paths and reducing abstraction honesty.
evidence: `DefaultEventRuntime()` returns global `defaultEventRuntime` (`goseg/docker/events/events.go` lines 36-40), and importer publishes directly through that singleton (`goseg/importer/importer.go` lines 365-367 and 405-410).
Broadcast state uses global `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and many APIs resolve to it implicitly (`goseg/broadcast/state.go` lines 16, 47-52, 127-158).
WiFi API exposes global `defaultWiFiRuntimeService` (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` lines 24-39), while other paths also permit custom runtime injection, yielding two abstraction routes.
findings.suggestion: Make runtime dependencies explicit at subsystem construction and thread them through callers; confine globals to bootstrap wiring only (or remove them), so production code uses the same injected path as tests.

findings.summary: The embedded SysConfig bag plus broad snapshot callbacks leaks cross-domain configuration concerns into orchestration code.
evidence: `SysConfig` embeds multiple domain configs directly (`goseg/structs/configs.go` lines 18-25), so call sites can access many domains through one model.

`RuntimeSnapshotOps` carries multiple unrelated snapshots (`ConfFn`, `StartramSettingsSnapshotFn`, `ShipSettingsSnapshotFn`, `Check502SettingsSnapshotFn`) in one dependency object (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` lines 165-172).
`main` C2C runtime bundles connectivity snapshots alongside mode/device behaviors (`goseg/main.go` lines 155-223), reflecting wide context bags instead of focused policies.
Config patch registry still mutates many domains through a central list (`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` lines 45+), reinforcing centralized cross-domain mutation.
findings.suggestion: Split configuration access by bounded context (connectivity/runtime/startram/etc.) and inject only the domain reader/updater needed by each workflow; replace `ConfFn`/wide snapshot bundles with context-specific ports.

findings.summary: Repeated default/with/resolve wrapper stacks add call depth with minimal policy value.
evidence: WiFi runtime construction chains through `DefaultWiFiRuntime` -> `defaultWiFiRuntime` -> `NewWiFiRuntimeWith` -> `mergeWiFiRuntime` and is mirrored in service constructors (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` lines 33-69, `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` lines 17-35).
Rectify runtime repeats `NewRectifyRuntime`/`defaultRectifyRuntime`/`withRectifyRuntimeDefaults` wrappers around dependency merge (`goseg/rectify/rectify.go` lines 34-59).
Broadcast runtime exposes `new`, `New`, `Default`, and `resolve` stack for the same state object (`goseg/broadcast/state.go` lines 30-52).
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a single constructor pattern per subsystem (for example `NewX(deps)` + optional `NewDefaultX()` only) and remove redundant resolve wrappers where they only forward values.

findings.summary: Urbit section-update API is duplicated across config and orchestration layers
evidence: `config/urbit.go` defines section constants, `UpdateUrbitSection*` wrappers, and `UrbitSectionMutator` switch for all sections.
`docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` redefines the same section constants and another set of per-section wrapper methods on `RuntimeUrbitConfigOps`, delegating into `config.UrbitSectionMutator`.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to one canonical section-update API. Keep section constants and section-specific wrappers in only one package (prefer `config`), and make orchestration depend on that API directly instead of re-exposing parallel wrappers.

findings.summary: Broadcast iteration logic is duplicated with unclear ownership in session subsystem
evidence: `sessionBroadcaster.broadcast` in `client_manager.go` iterates token buckets, writes to active clients, invokes failure callback, and updates `LastActive`.
`ConnectionRegistry.broadcast` in `connection_registry.go` repeats the same broadcast loop structure and side effects, but is separate from the broadcaster path used by `ClientManager`.
findings.suggestion: Choose one owner for websocket fan-out behavior (either broadcaster or registry) and remove the duplicate implementation. Keep registry focused on state/indexing and move all send/failure policy into a single broadcast component.

findings.summary: Urbit transition handlers mix orchestration, persistence, and external side effects
evidence: `buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps` combines runtime state derivation, click/docker calls, config mutation, and transition event staging in one function.
`buildUrbitDomainSteps`/`buildMinIODomainSteps` combine alias policy checks, external startram mutations, and config writes inside transition step closures.
`rectify/rectify.go` contains separate reducer/router orchestration for related transition domains, indicating orchestration responsibilities are spread across multiple surfaces.
findings.suggestion: Extract transition use-cases into service functions with explicit phases (validate, execute side effects, persist state, emit transition events). Keep step builders declarative by calling those services rather than embedding domain logic inline.

findings.summary: Schema evolution requires edits in multiple large manual switch maps
evidence: `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` in `structs/configs.go` is a long key-by-key switch with defaulting/parsing logic interleaved.
`UrbitSectionMutator` in `config/urbit.go` is another large section switch with repeated type assertion and mutation-wrapper construction.
Both switches encode schema knowledge separately, so adding/changing fields risks multi-file drift.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single schema registry/descriptor table that maps keys/sections to typed handlers, then generate both unmarshal and section-mutation dispatch from that registry to eliminate parallel switch maintenance.

findings.summary: Transition event publish failures are silently ignored across runtime paths despite broker-defined failure modes.
evidence: `EventRuntime.Publish*Transition` returns concrete errors for backpressure/cancellation (`ErrTransitionBusFull`, `ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`) in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`.
`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` discards publish errors with `_ = ...PublishUrbitTransition(...)` at lines 70 and 362.

`goseg/importer/importer.go` publishes import transitions in `Reset`, `publishImportStatus`, `publishImportError`, `publishUploadStatus`, and `monitorExtractionProgress` without handling returned errors.

`goseg/handler/ship/backup.go` publishes multiple transition states (loading/success/error/clear) and ignores publish results, so UI/event consumers can miss status updates without any surfaced error.
findings.suggestion: Define and apply a single transition publish policy: either (a) propagate publish errors at operation boundaries, or (b) centralize best-effort publish with structured logging/metrics and explicit rationale. Replace raw `_ = Publish...` and unchecked publish calls with a shared helper that records dropped transition events consistently.

findings.summary: Config decode paths disagree on invalid-type handling: one fails fast, another silently coerces to zero values.
evidence: `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` in `goseg/structs/configs.go` uses unchecked assertions (`field, _ = v.(type)`) for many fields, so malformed JSON types are silently converted to defaults/zero values with no error.
`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` parsers (`parseBoolValue`, `parseIntValue`, `parseStringValue`, etc.) explicitly return typed errors like `invalid <name> value: <type>`.
Because both files participate in config ingestion/update behavior, callers get different failure modes for similar bad input, making errors unpredictable and harder to diagnose.
findings.suggestion: Unify config error semantics: convert `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` to strict typed parsing (returning contextual errors on mismatches), or explicitly document/implement lenient parsing in both paths with consistent warning emission. Prefer a shared parsing utility to avoid divergent behavior.
